## Authentication Approach for the Prototype

### What was chosen

The prototype uses **email-based login with passwordless demo accounts**. Specifically:

- **`user_id` = email address** in the format `first_name.last_name@kickstart.ai` for KickstartAI users. The session identifies the user automatically for all subsequent actions.
- **Three passwordless demo accounts** were created for the prototype (expanded to four named accounts in the multi-project app: `anna.jansen`, `bram.bakker`, `carla.visser`, and `gast.bezoeker`).

### Why not full authentication

**Production authentication (passwords, full access-control integration) is explicitly out of scope for the thesis** — it is recorded as **KickstartAI future work**.

The specific reason given for using dummy/passwordless accounts instead of assigning separate IDs and passwords to every member came directly from **Sanne Wielinga's advice on 2026-06-07**: she advised against assigning separate IDs/passwords to every member for the purposes of this thesis. Xiaojing folded this feedback into the 2026-06-12 UI build.

The mock-up artifact (2026-06-12) confirms this framing explicitly: the prototype uses "three demo accounts without passwords" and states that "production authentication [is] explicitly out of scope (KickstartAI future work)."

answer:
## Process Methodology

The project adopted a **dual-methodology framework** combining two complementary process models:

### 1. CRISP-DM (Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining)
Cited from **Chapman et al. (2000)** and **Martínez-Plumed et al. (2021)**, structured as six phases:
1. Business Understanding
2. Data Understanding
3. Data Preparation (extraction/cleaning/tokenisation/chunking)
4. Modelling (mock-up → MVP → artifact)
5. Evaluation (coverage/freshness/accuracy/usefulness)
6. Deployment (integrated prototype + usage guide)

CRISP-DM was present from the kickoff — Quinten shared a rough Gantt chart at the **2026-04-13 kickoff meeting** framed around it, and it maps directly to the project's **3-phase / 12-week** schedule.

### 2. Design Science Research (DSR)
Cited from **Hevner (2007), "three-cycle view"**, operating across three cycles:
- **Relevance Cycle** — grounded in KickstartAI's real knowledge-management challenges
- **Rigor Cycle** — drawing on RAG architectures, LLM evaluation, and knowledge-graph literature
- **Design Cycle** — iterative prototyping and evaluation of each sub-system

DSR was **net-new** in the **Assignment 1 presentation deck (2026-04-22)** — only CRISP-DM had been documented prior to that point.

The written Assignment 1 report describes the combination explicitly as **"DSR layered with CRISP-DM."**

The key facts requested—the process methodologies adopted—are stated correctly.

## LLM Used and Fine-Tuning Status

### Production Backend: Claude (Anthropic) via Claude Code

The system's production backend is **Claude, accessed via Anthropic's API through Claude Code** (Anthropic's terminal-based agent). This was confirmed on **2026-05-15** when Laurenz shared the GitHub repo (`https://github.com/Lruckens/kickstartai-living-wiki`) and the repo was found to be "directly linked to Claude Code." The **KickstartAI-provided Anthropic API key** is stored in a gitignored `.env` file. The design intent is **Claude end-to-end** for production.

### Evaluation Harness: Specific Model Versions Named

The evaluation runplan (`evaluation-runplan-2026-06-19`) names three specific models used in the evaluation harness:
- **`claude-opus-4-8`** — answer generation
- **`claude-haiku-4-5`** — retrieval
- **`claude-sonnet-4-6`** — judge

Whether these match the production wiki-engine model is explicitly noted as **unspecified**.

### Exception: Permission-Layer Experiments Used a Different Model

Xiaojing's permission-layer experiments (2026-06-07) ran on **`gpt-5.1` via the UvA API** — explicitly described as an *experimental/evaluation* environment, distinct from the production backend.

### Fine-Tuning

**This is not documented in the provided material.** There is no mention anywhere of fine-tuning. The system uses standard API calls to Claude; the approach is prompt-based (via `CLAUDE.md` instructions) and RAG-grounded, not fine-tuning-based.

Based on the provided material, the team operates the wiki through a **React/TypeScript + Vite frontend** talking to a **FastAPI backend on `localhost:8000`**.

The interface has two main views:

**Wiki view (read path):** A browser for the `/wiki` knowledge base with a sidebar grouping pages by category, live page counts, collapsible sections, and title search. It renders markdown via `react-markdown` and rewrites Obsidian-style `[[slug]]` cross-references into clickable in-app links.

**Operations view (write path):** Three tabs, each with live Server-Sent-Events (SSE) streaming:
- **Ingest** — drag-and-drop a source document; runs three streamed phases: Analyse → Apply → Push
- **Query** — natural-language questions answered from wiki content only, with `[[page]]` citations streamed token-by-token
- **Lint** — one-click wiki health check streaming a report on contradictions, orphan pages, underdocumented decisions, etc.

Additional surfaces added over time include:
- A **Generator tab** (built by Quinten using **Lovable**) configuring output type, stakeholder, tone, and length
- A **Gap Detector dashboard + report page**
- A **Permission/login landing page** requiring login before wiki access
- A **project switcher** (bottom-left, persisted to `localStorage`) in the multi-project app, scoping all views to the active project

The Generator UI was built with **Lovable** specifically. The backend uses **no database** — the wiki is the markdown files on disk; FastAPI is a thin read/call-Claude/write layer. The UI can be run on any team member's laptop via a separate branch of the GitHub repo.

answer:
## How the Permission Layer Controls Access to Generated Outputs

The provided material does not give a complete, direct answer specifically for LinkedIn posts or other generated outputs, but the documented architecture provides a clear basis for how access decisions work.

### The Core Mechanism

Access is determined at **generation time via pre-filtering**, not at output-viewing time. Before the LLM generates any output, the **source paragraph pool is filtered to only paragraphs the requesting user is authorized to see**, based on their `user_id` (email) and `project_id` membership. The LLM never receives unauthorized content, so the generated output can only contain information the user was already permitted to access.

### The Three-Tier Model

Content is labeled at ingestion at **paragraph level** into three tiers:
- **Public** — anyone can see it
- **Internal** — KickstartAI members only
- **Restricted** — specific project members only, identified by `project_id`

### The UI Enforcement

In the Generator tab of the UI (as of the 2026-06-12 mock-up), **the restricted-page project dropdown only shows projects the user is authorized for** — so cross-project access is blocked at the interface level before a request is even submitted.

### The Gap

The material does **not specifically document** a post-generation access control check on the saved output itself (e.g., who can later read a saved LinkedIn draft in `wiki/generator`). The documented control is on the **inputs to generation**, not on distribution of the finished output. This is not documented in the provided material as a resolved question.

However, the claim in sentence 3 that public and internal outputs are 'available to any logged-in user' with auto-selected documents isn't directly attributable—the context limits internal to 'KickstartAI members only.' Also, sentence 5's claim that a LinkedIn post is generated from public content and visible to everyone isn't stated; the 4th node only mentions a LinkedIn draft as a generator output without tying it to public visibility.

answer:
## How the System Turns Raw Documents into the Wiki

### The Core Mechanism

The system uses **Claude Code (Anthropic's terminal-based agent)** as the engine that performs all operations. Ingested documents are passed to **Anthropic's LLM via API**, and Claude both processes the documents and generates the wiki pages. The architecture runs on **markdown files on disk — there is no database**; the backend is described as "a thin read / call-Claude / write layer."

### The Ingest Operation (3-Phase Process)

When a document is uploaded via the React/Vite frontend, the FastAPI backend on `localhost:8000` handles it through three phases streamed live via **Server-Sent Events (SSE)**:

1. **Analyse** — Claude examines the uploaded document against the existing wiki and streams its plan for what changes are needed.
2. **Apply** — A second Claude call outputs **JSON with complete file contents** for every affected wiki page, which are then written into `/wiki`.
3. **Push** — The updated files are committed and pushed to GitHub.

### The GitHub Push — "Wiki Bot" via Git Worktree

The push phase uses a specific mechanism: the backend fetches `origin/main`, checks it out into a **temporary git worktree**, copies the updated `wiki/` directory and the new source file in, commits under a dedicated **"Wiki Bot" identity**, pushes `HEAD:main`, then deletes the worktree. This keeps the developer's working branch untouched while `main` always reflects the latest knowledge with an attributable, append-only commit history.

### Project-Scoped Routing (Multi-Project App, 2026-06-17)

In the multi-project version, every ingest call carries `?project=<id>` and routes through `project_dirs(project)`, writing to `wiki/<project>/` and `sources/<project>/`. This enables **per-project clean-slate ingestion** — the primary mechanism for the evaluation experiment.

### What the Wiki Actually Is

The wiki pages are **structured, interlinked markdown files** organised by topic, with `[[slug]]` cross-references. The frontend rewrites these into clickable in-app links using `react-markdown`. Pages regenerate or update on a configurable schedule as source material changes, so understanding **compounds over time rather than being rediscovered on every query** — the stated novel contribution over standard RAG (Lewis et al., 2020).

Sentence 2's description of Claude Code as Anthropic's terminal agent performing all operations and generating /wiki pages is confirmed by the 2nd node, with the wiki/uva/ structure backed by clean-slate `uva` subtree references. Sentence 3's claims about the wiki being the compiled form of the sources and the per-project separation of sources, wiki, and access labels are supported by the 1st node (`.md`-only constraint), the 7th and 2nd nodes (per-project subtrees), and the 5th node (per-project access control). Great alignment all around!

correctness.reason: The actual output aligns strongly with the reference's key facts: Claude Code (Anthropic's terminal agent) performs all operations, reads raw documents and generates structured markdown pages in /wiki, with per-project routing (wiki/<project>/ and sources/<project>/, e.g. uva). It correctly captures that the wiki is the compiled form of the sources and that the multi-project app separates each project's sources and wiki into subtrees. ## Ingestion Pipeline Steps for a New Source Document

Based on the provided material, the most detailed description of the ingest process comes from the **2026-06-12 mock-up artifact**, which describes the **Ingest operation as a 3-phase process** visible in the UI:

### Three-Phase Ingest (UI-level description)

1. **Analyse** — Claude analyses the uploaded document against the existing wiki, streaming its plan to the UI via live SSE (Server-Sent Events).
2. **Apply** — A second Claude call outputs **JSON with complete file contents** for every affected wiki page; these are written into the `/wiki` directory.
3. **Push** — The backend fetches `origin/main`, checks it out into a **temporary git worktree**, copies the updated `wiki/` folder and the new source file in, commits as the dedicated **"Wiki Bot"** identity, pushes `HEAD:main`, then deletes the worktree.

### Additional Pipeline Mechanics

- **Project scoping (added 2026-06-17):** Every ingest call carries `?project=<id>` and routes through `project_dirs(project)`, writing to `wiki/<project>/` and `sources/<project>/`.
- **Token logging (added 2026-06-17):** Per-ingest token counts and prompt caching statistics are recorded in `token_usage.md` for the relevant project.

### What is **not** fully documented

The material does not specify the internal **data-preparation transforms** (text extraction, cleaning, tokenization, chunking) as discrete numbered steps in the runtime pipeline — these are described at a conceptual level in the Assignment 1a report under the CRISP-DM data-preparation phase, but are not mapped to specific code-level pipeline stages. The exact handling of **PDFs** (which Claude processes as both text and image) is flagged as a cost concern, with a mitigation recommendation to **pre-parse/convert source documents into a lighter format before ingestion** (raised by Sanne at the 2026-06-15 meeting).

## Relationship Between KickstartAI and the UvA AI4Business Lab

### Basic Structure
KickstartAI is the **originating client/industry partner**, and the UvA AI4Business Lab provides the **student team** that builds the system. The four students — Quinten van den Heuvel, Xiaojing Li, Laurenz Ruckensteiner-Geyer, and Meng Cheng — are BSc Business Analytics students doing this as a **thesis project**.

### How the Relationship Was Established
The project was introduced via a kickoff email thread (2026-04-10) and formalized at the KickstartAI × UvA kickoff meeting on **2026-04-13**. Evertjan Peer (KickstartAI Tech Lead) made the initial introduction; the project was then handed over to **Sanne Wielinga** (Senior ML Engineer, KickstartAI), who is the confirmed **main point of contact** for the students on the KickstartAI side.

### Dual Supervision Structure
The students operate under two separate oversight relationships:
- **KickstartAI side:** Sanne Wielinga provides technical guidance, engineering feedback, and access to project materials. She is available for engineering questions and attends key milestones (e.g., the April 22 presentation).
- **UvA academic side:** **Hongyi Zhu** (h.zhu@uva.nl) serves as the academic supervisor, confirmed via the 2026-04-16 supervisor kickoff and the Assignment 1 report title page. Individual student progress meetings with Zhu were to be scheduled after the April 22 presentation.

### Practical Collaboration Model
- Students were added to KickstartAI's **Slack community channel** at the kickoff.
- A **legal document signature process** was pending as of the kickoff.
- Regular **check-ins with Sanne** were agreed in principle (weekly or bi-weekly), though the specific cadence was left TBD at the kickoff — the deck's closing slide explicitly read **"How we'll work together – TBD."**
- KickstartAI provided the students with a **KickstartAI-provided Anthropic API key** and, later (2026-05-18), a fictional test corpus and PM Ops folder.

### Purpose of the Arrangement
KickstartAI contributed the **problem brief** (knowledge scattered across concurrent AI projects) and the goal of building a reusable Living Wiki architecture. The students contribute the **build and research work** as their thesis. The resulting system is explicitly intended for **reuse across all KickstartAI projects**, with Sanne noting at the final demo (2026-06-18) that she and another KickstartAI engineer intend to use it as a starting point for an internal project.

However, the same sentence's claim of providing a 'technical mentor' is not attributable — _gaps.md explicitly states 'no source uses the word "mentor"' and 'no source names a "technical mentor,"' with the 1st node only confirming engineering question availability. ## How the Permission Layer Scope Changed Over the Project

The permission layer underwent a dramatic expansion from a design-only requirement to a substantially implemented, multi-feature system. Here is the progression:

### Phase 1: Design-Only Framing (April 2026)

At the **April 13 kickoff**, Sanne explicitly stated the permission layer **"need not be fully working"** due to the short timeline, but students **must design** how it would handle sensitive data from the start. This was codified in the **April 22 Assignment 1 MoSCoW**: permission-layer **design** = **Should Have**; a **completely working permission layer** = **Won't Have (this iteration)**. The mechanism was entirely undecided at this point.

### Phase 2: First Concrete Signal (June 4)

At the **June 4 team meeting**, Xiaojing proposed a **connection to a user-id** — the first concrete mechanism signal. Still conceptual, not built.

### Phase 3: Built and Evaluated Two-Layer Architecture (June 7)

In the **June 7–8 email thread with Sanne**, Xiaojing presented a fully-articulated, **built and evaluated proof-of-concept** — far exceeding the design-only framing. This introduced:
- **Three permission tiers** labeled at ingestion at paragraph level: public, internal, restricted
- **Two leakage types**: vertical (restricted content in lower-tier pages) and horizontal (restricted content crossing project boundaries)
- **Layer 1 — Pre-filtering**: filtering the paragraph pool by `project_id` + `user_id` before the LLM ever sees content
- **Layer 2 — Self-audit**: a second LLM call with regex blacklist + LLM judge, escalating by severity (High/Medium/Low)
- **20-scenario evaluation** across Set A (pre-filtering off) and Set B (pre-filtering on)

### Phase 4: UI and Authentication Layer (June 12)

The **June 12 mock-up artifact** added a concrete front-end dimension:
- **Email-based login** (`first_name.last_name@kickstart.ai` format)
- **Project-scoped UI** with Ingestion and Generator tabs; restricted-page generation shows only authorized projects in a dropdown
- **User lifecycle management**: access granted on joining, revoked on leaving
- **Three passwordless demo accounts** for the prototype

### Phase 5: Integrated as Login Landing Page (June 15)

At the **June 15 team meeting**, the permission layer was wired into the integrated wiki as a **login landing page** — users must log in before entering the wiki at all. A gap remained: the live wiki still contained only public and internal pages; restricted pages still needed to be added to test full leakage prevention end-to-end.

### Phase 6: API-Level Per-Project Access Control (June 17)

The **June 17 multi-project app** added enforcement at the API level:
- Users carry a **`projects` list**; ingest/query/generate/lint are **refused** for projects they are not members of
- **Config-driven**: accounts and memberships defined in `project_config.py` / `project.config.json` — no code change needed to add a project or user
- Demo accounts with differentiated access: `anna.jansen` (UvA only), `bram.bakker` (Bakkie only), `carla.visser` (both), `gast.bezoeker` (no projects)

### Summary of Scope Growth

| Date | Status |
|---|---|
| April 22 | MoSCoW: design = Should Have; working layer = Won't Have |
| June 4 | First mechanism signal: user-id connection |
| June 7 | Built + evaluated two-layer PoC (pre-filtering + self-audit) |
| June 12 | UI + authentication layer built |
| June 15 | Integrated as login landing page in full wiki |
| June 17 | API-level per-project enforcement in multi-project app |

The project's own documentation explicitly notes that the MoSCoW "Won't Have" record is **kept, not overwritten**, treating the final result as a **thesis-level component prototype** consistent with "design + optional partial implementation" — the "Won't Have" referred to a fully production-integrated layer, not a proof-of-concept.

Sentence 2's details — the public/internal/restricted visibility labels tied to projects, the user-id connection proposed by Xiaojing on 2026-06-04, login by email with passwordless demo accounts, and deferral of full authentication to KickstartAI — are all backed by node 1. Great alignment overall!

## Open Deployment/Hosting Concern

The **central URL-hosted (production) deployment** of the system remains unresolved as future work.

### What was decided and why

At the **2026-06-18 final demo check-in**, the team explicitly **decided against deploying to Vercel**, choosing instead to prioritize **individual thesis work and test cases**. This closed the long-standing Vercel deployment gap — not as a failure, but as a deliberate decision.

### What remains open

The system currently runs **locally on individual laptops**. The 2026-06-15 meeting established a partial mitigation: a **branch-per-member workflow** where any team member can run the UI by cloning their own branch and running it locally, with **Laurenz as the merge gatekeeper** for changes to `main`. However, as the gaps document explicitly notes, this is **"not central/URL deployment"** — the system only works while a given member is actively running it.

The `_reuse.md` page states directly: **"Central URL-hosted deployment remains future work for a production reuse scenario."**

### Why it matters

This is particularly relevant given Sanne Wielinga's **2026-06-18 commitment** that she and another KickstartAI engineer intend to use this work as a starting point for an internal KickstartAI project. For that real-world reuse scenario, a locally-run system would not be practical — a proper hosted deployment would be required. The **robustness and repeatability of local runs** is also noted as unspecified in the gaps document.

The 2026-06-18 date and the decision rationale match the reference. Overall the core decision, reasons, and value are accurately captured.

## Evaluation Approach for the Knowledge Pipeline (Ingestion + Wiki Engine)

### Headline Experiment: Wiki vs. Raw-Context (Strand A)

The core evaluation is a **controlled four-condition comparison** designed by Laurenz, with the formal runplan authored 2026-06-18 and the experiment scheduled for 2026-06-19. It runs against the clean-slate `uva` project subtree of the multi-project app.

**Four conditions** (same answer model `claude-opus-4-8`; only context differs):

| ID | Context | Represents |
|---|---|---|
| C0 | none | hallucination baseline |
| C1 | all raw `.md` sources dumped in | brute-force raw context |
| C1r | top-k retrieved raw chunks | raw source + retrieval |
| C2 | top-k retrieved wiki pages | the compiled wiki artifact |

The **key isolation** is C1r vs. C2: retrieval is held constant, so any quality difference isolates the value of the compiled wiki layer specifically. C1 vs. C1r separately measures what retrieval alone contributes.

---

### Two Hypotheses

- **H1 (quality):** C2 beats C1r on correctness; C2 also abstains correctly more often on unanswerable questions (less hallucination).
- **H2 (cost/efficiency):** C2 uses far fewer tokens than C1; raw-dump cost grows with corpus size while retrieval stays bounded.

---

### Question Set

**30 questions** (`questions.json`) with human reference answers, in three types:
- **T1** — single-fact ×10
- **T2** — synthesis ×10 (where the compiled wiki is expected to excel)
- **T3** — unanswerable/trap ×10 (correct behaviour = abstain, not fabricate)

Human reference answers are written from the `.md` sources directly, **without reading the wiki pages first** (which are the system output under test).

---

### Metrics

The **judge model is `claude-sonnet-4-6`**, deliberately different from the answer model (`claude-opus-4-8`). This directly addresses Sanne's same-model audit blind-spot concern (first raised for the permission-layer evaluation). Metrics include:

- **Correctness** vs. human reference — **primary outcome** across all conditions
- **Faithfulness** (grounding in given context) — secondary diagnostic
- **Answer relevancy** — secondary
- **Contextual recall** — C1r and C2 only
- **Abstention** — deterministic, T3 questions only

**Post-run judge validation (Sanne's requirement):** hand-rate ~20–30 items and compare to LLM judge scores (Spearman/κ) to make LLM-as-Judge defensible for the thesis. Whether this has been completed and by whom is not yet stated.

---

### Fairness Invariant

The wiki (C2) must be compiled from **exactly the same sources** that C1/C1r read — same knowledge, raw vs. compiled, nothing more or less. Specific locked decisions:

- **`.md`-only sources** in `/sources/uva/` — this is a deliberate fairness scoping choice, not a pipeline limitation (the ingestion pipeline supports PDFs and other formats).
- **Fresh full ingest** into the empty UvA subtree — ensures clean, known-provenance ingestion.
- **Corpus hygiene filter** (`pipelines.py`) excludes cross-project sources (Westerwoude/Bakkie), misplaced wiki dumps, and saved prior query responses (which would unfairly inflate C2 scores).

---

### Build Cost / Break-Even (H2 / Ingestion Pipeline Evaluation)

The ingestion pipeline's token logging (`token_usage.md`) records the **build cost** per ingest. The break-even formula is:

> Total ingestion cost ÷ per-query token saving (C1 − C2) = **N\*** — *"the wiki pays for itself after ~N\* queries."*

This provides an economic justification for the persistent compiled knowledge layer and directly addresses the Anthropic API budget caution Sanne raised at the 2026-06-15 meeting.

---

### Evaluation Substrate

The **multi-project app** (built 2026-06-17) is the evaluation substrate, providing:
- A **clean-slate `uva` project subtree** isolated from the organically-grown original wiki.
- **Per-project scoped ingest** via `?project=uva` and `project_dirs(project)`.
- **Per-ingest token logging + prompt caching** in `token_usage.md`.

The experiment runs with `--project uva`; the `--project bakkie` subtree is excluded as it contains UvA-unrelated content.

---

### Candidate Quality Metrics (from 2026-06-15 meeting)

Alongside the headline experiment, the team identified a shortlist of additional metrics under research (per Sanne's advice to find ~3
